similarPlantWarning: Often confused with wild sage or tea bush.
symptomsText: Photosensitization, jaundice, loss of appetite, constipation, followed by severe diarrhea, swollen muzzle and ears.
firstAid: Move the animal to a shaded, dark area immediately to prevent photosensitive skin burns. Provide plenty of fresh water and electrolytes.
treatment: Administer activated charcoal (2-4 g/kg body weight) via stomach tube. Fluid therapy and supportive treatment by a veterinarian.
warningMessage: Extremely toxic to cattle! Consuming even a small amount can lead to liver failure and death.

similarPlantWarning: Confused with white top or wild carrot.
symptomsText: Severe dermatitis, loss of hair, skin lesions, inflammation of muzzle, diarrhea, decreased milk yield, and respiratory issues.
firstAid: Wash affected skin areas with mild soap and water. Keep the animal away from parthenium infested pastures.
treatment: Antihistamines and topical corticosteroid creams for skin irritation. Supportive therapy for digestive symptoms.
warningMessage: Causes severe allergic contact dermatitis in both livestock and humans. Keep livestock away.

similarPlantWarning: Often confused with surrounding non-toxic green pastures.
symptomsText: Digestive distress, mild colic, decreased feeding, salivation.
firstAid: Isolate the animal immediately. Give fresh clean water. Contact vet if symptoms worsen.
treatment: Provide supportive care, fluids, and anti-inflammatories as recommended by a vet.
warningMessage: Keep livestock away from areas infested with Crown Flower (Calotropis).
